aliases: 奇亚籽布丁 (Qí Yà Zǐ Bùdīng)

chinese: 奇亚籽布丁 (Qí Yà Zǐ Bùdīng)

region_of_origin: Central America (Salvia hispanica origin); globally popularised from approximately 2012; widely available in Singapore health cafés
food_subgroup: Seed-based pudding; chia seeds soaked overnight in unsweetened almond milk, set into a thick pudding-like consistency, topped with fresh mango and honey

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has chia seeds (FOODON_03315091) as a food product but does not have a specific entry for chia seed pudding as a prepared dish. Closest parent is seed-based pudding or chia seed food product. URI used is for the chia seed ingredient anchor only.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chia seeds — FoodEx2 base term for Salvia hispanica seeds; used as closest defensible anchor for this chia-seed-based pudding preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chia seed pudding as a composite prepared dish. A00LC (chia seeds) is used as the closest defensible ingredient-level anchor. The prepared pudding includes almond milk, mango, and honey as additional components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical finding or food substance code identified for chia seed pudding as a prepared dish. Not clinically coded at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one jar or bowl of approximately 320g, comprising 30g chia seeds, 200ml unsweetened almond milk, 80g fresh mango, 5g honey, and 5g garnish, as commonly served at Singapore health cafés.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chia seeds (SR Legacy #12006), Almond milk unsweetened (SR Legacy), Mango raw (SR Legacy #09176), Honey (SR Legacy #19296); composite calculation for prepared pudding
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — FoodData Central / SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for the prepared pudding (per 100g) are derived by weighted composite calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for chia seeds, unsweetened almond milk, raw mango, and honey, in proportions matching the standard 320g serving (30g chia seeds + 200ml almond milk + 80g mango + 5g honey + 5g garnish). No single USDA entry exists for chia seed pudding as a composite prepared dish. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of chia seeds and chia-containing foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Vuksan et al. (2010) 'Supplementation of conventional therapy with the novel grain Salvia hispanica L. improves major and emerging cardiovascular risk factors in type 2 diabetes', Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chia seeds have a GI of approximately 1 (essentially non-glycaemic) per University of Sydney GI Database, owing to 34.4g dietary fibre per 100g dry weight forming a viscous gel that physically impedes carbohydrate absorption. The composite pudding GI of ~38 is an analogous estimate accounting for the glycaemic contribution of mango (GI ~51) and honey (GI ~58) diluted within the 320g serving. Glycaemic load of 22.5 is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (38 × 59.2g) / 100 ≈ 22.5. This is consistent with the input record and supported by Vuksan et al. (2010) demonstrating chia's GI-lowering effect on co-consumed carbohydrates.

hypertension.key_factor: High magnesium (62mg/100g prepared; ~335mg/100g dry chia), high potassium (162mg/100g), very low sodium (42mg/100g), and omega-3 ALA with cardioprotective and vasodilatory effects
hypertension.guidance: Chia seed pudding is beneficial for hypertension management: its high magnesium content supports vascular smooth muscle relaxation, potassium from chia and mango promotes sodium excretion, very low sodium content (42mg/100g) is well within hypertension dietary targets, and omega-3 ALA (approximately 5.3g per 30g chia serving) provides cardioprotective anti-inflammatory benefit; suitable as a regular breakfast choice for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low composite GI (~38), high dietary fibre (5.5g/100g prepared; ~17.6g per 320g serving), and chia gel forming a physical barrier to carbohydrate absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chia seed pudding is an excellent breakfast choice for Type 2 Diabetes management: the viscous chia gel (formed by hydrophilic polysaccharides) physically slows carbohydrate digestion and absorption, resulting in a low composite GI of approximately 38 and a glycaemic load of 22.5 per 320g serving; the high fibre content (17.6g per serving) further blunts postprandial glucose response; honey can be omitted to reduce GL to approximately 18 for tighter glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High plant-based ALA omega-3 (approximately 5.3g per 30g chia serving), high dietary fibre, low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chia seed pudding is cardioprotective: chia seeds provide the highest plant-based ALA omega-3 content of any common food (17.8g/100g dry weight), contributing approximately 5.3g ALA per standard serving, which supports reduction of triglycerides and systemic inflammation; the very low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (17.6g per serving), and high magnesium content collectively support cardiovascular risk reduction; suitable as a regular breakfast for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High ALA omega-3 anti-inflammatory effect, high magnesium, and calcium supporting bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chia seed pudding is highly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis: the high ALA omega-3 content (5.3g per 30g chia serving) provides meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit, magnesium (62mg/100g prepared) supports muscle function and has anti-inflammatory properties, and calcium (115mg/100g) from chia and almond milk supports bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chia Seed Pudding (奇亚籽布丁) is one of the most nutritionally dense breakfast preparations available in Singapore's health food scene. It is made by soaking whole chia seeds (Salvia hispanica) in unsweetened almond milk overnight without any cooking. Chia seeds absorb approximately 10 times their weight in liquid, forming a thick, tapioca-like gel that sets into a pudding with the texture of soft tapioca pearls or thick rice pudding. The standard Singapore café serving (320g) comprises 30g chia seeds, 200ml unsweetened almond milk, 80g fresh mango, and 5g honey. Chia seeds are nutritionally exceptional: they are the highest plant-based source of ALA omega-3 fatty acids (17.8g/100g dry weight), contain extraordinary dietary fibre (34.4g/100g dry — among the highest of any commonly consumed food), provide complete protein with all essential amino acids, and are rich in magnesium (335mg/100g dry), calcium, phosphorus, zinc, and iron. The prepared pudding per 100g delivers 132 kcal, 4.2g protein, 4.5g fat, 18.5g carbohydrate, 5.5g dietary fibre, and 42mg sodium. The composite glycaemic index of the pudding is approximately 38 (low), as the viscous chia gel forms a physical barrier to carbohydrate absorption in the gut, reducing the glycaemic impact of co-consumed mango and honey. This makes it a gold-standard breakfast for Type 2 Diabetes management, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease prevention. It is vegetarian, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal. Available at Singapore health cafés including Grain, Hvala, and Chilla, and straightforward to prepare at home with zero cooking required. Particularly relevant for vegan or dairy-free patients seeking anti-inflammatory omega-3 without marine sources.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy component data for all four main ingredients (chia seeds, unsweetened almond milk, raw mango, honey) via weighted composite calculation consistent with the standard 320g serving. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-validated against known USDA SR Legacy data for chia seeds (SR Legacy #12006). GI estimate is supported by University of Sydney GI Database data for chia seeds and peer-reviewed literature (Vuksan et al. 2010). Multilingual names, ontology anchors, and health context are complete and defensible.
notes: water_g set to -1: detailed moisture content for the composite prepared pudding (chia seeds + almond milk + mango + honey in overnight-set form) is not directly reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation; moisture is implicitly high given the liquid absorption by chia seeds but a precise per-100g figure for the composite dish is not available from approved sources as of 2026-04-13. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for all component ingredients). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based with no animal-derived cholesterol). monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.2g/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for chia seeds and almond milk in the composite proportion; these are approximate values. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.1 (trace level from fortified almond milk; value is plausible but dependent on specific almond milk brand fortification). GI of 38 is a composite analogous estimate; the chia seed component has GI ~1 per University of Sydney GI Database, with the composite GI elevated by mango and honey contributions. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because honey is included; the dish is vegetarian but not strictly vegan. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible ingredient-level anchor for chia seeds; no composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 for chia seed pudding.
